style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Approval</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">{<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">attribute</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approved</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Boolean</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">attribute</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approver</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">String</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Creating metadata definition elements via the Create Element command</h4><p>To create metadata definition elements via the Create Element command</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Element</strong>.</p></li><li>In the <strong>Create Element</strong> dialog, select the <strong>metadata def</strong> element.</li><li>Name the element.</li><li>Specify the metadata attributes.</li></ol><h4>Creating metadata definition elements via the view palette</h4><p>To create metadata definition elements via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Items/Parts/Attributes group, click the<span> <strong>metadata </strong></span><strong>def </strong>button to create a metadata definition element.</li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the element.</li><li>Specify the metadata attributes.</li></ol><h3><span>Applying metadata to an element</span> </h3><h4>Applying metadata with attributes to an element via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To apply metadata with attributes to an element via the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Textual Editor, within the body of the element for which you want to apply metadata, do one of the following:</p><ol><li>For unnamed metadata usage, do one of the following:<ol><li>Declare the <em>@</em> symbol followed by the metadata definition element name.</li><li>Declare the keyword <em>metadata</em><em> </em>followed by the metadata definition name.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ad541e78-feb8-4e50-bcce-919eadaf772a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The notational difference between using the textual symbol @ or the keyword <em>metadata</em> depends on the <strong>Display Metadata as Keywords</strong> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Environment options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[environment option]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. By default, it is set to <em>false</em>, thus the notational representation of metadata in the textual notation is set to the textual symbol @. Even if you use a different notation, once the Textual Editor is reopened, the notational representation is changed to the one corresponding to the environment option. Set the environment option to <em>true</em> to use the keyword <em>metadata </em>notational representation instead.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">For a named metadata usage element:</p><ol><li>Declare the <em>@</em> symbol followed by the element name, and then define it by the metadata definition.</li><li>Declare the keyword <em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="776993b9-3949-4cd2-9feb-3358f65ab9d9">metadata, </ac:inline-comment-marker></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="776993b9-3949-4cd2-9feb-3358f65ab9d9">specify the element name, and then define </ac:inline-comment-marker>it by the metadata definition.</li></ol></li></ol></li><li>Specify the metadata attribute values in the element body.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94b2a80c-81dd-4880-80ad-76f2fd16628b"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// unnamed metadata usage defined by metadata definition:</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">system</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">{</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   <span>@</span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Approval</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">{   <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the at sign @ symbol followed by the metadata definition element name (step 1.a.i)</span><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">     <span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approved</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">=<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">true</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br style="text-align: left;" /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">     <span> </span></span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approver</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">=<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">&quot;John Smith&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br style="text-align: left;" /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">/* or */</span><br style="text-align: left;" /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span>   </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">metadata</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Approval</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">{   <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the keyword <em>metadata</em> followed by the metadata definition name (<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">step 1.a.ii)<br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">     <span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approved</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">=<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">true</span>;<br style="text-align: left;" /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">     <span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approver</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">=<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">&quot;John Smith&quot;</span>;<br style="text-align: left;" />    }</span></span></span><br style="text-align: left;" /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// named metadata usage defined by metadata definition:</span></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">system</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    @</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approvalSystem</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Approval</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> { <strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">  </span></strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the at sign @ symbol followed by the metadata usage name, defined by the metadata definition (step 1.a.i)</span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">       </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approved</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">true</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">       </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approver</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">&quot;John Smith&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">metadata</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approvalSystem</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Approval</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">the keyword <em>metadata</em> followed by the metadata usage name, defined by metadata definition (step 1.b.ii)</span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approved</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">true</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approver</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">&quot;John Smith&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Applying metadata without attributes to an element via the Textual Editor</h4><p>Using the following notation applies the metadata to an element without the possibility of specifying the metadata attributes.</p><p>To apply metadata without attributes to an element via the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Textual Editor, prior to the element keyword of the element you want to apply the metadata to, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c598f3c0-f3a3-4e8a-90c2-88f9bd8e29cc">type the hash sign</ac:inline-comment-marker> # followed by the metadata definition element name. </p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6650bfbc-5d84-445d-85fe-f01a67f7bdaf"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">#</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Approval</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> system;</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Applying metadata to an element via the Drag and Drop handler in a view </h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To apply metadata to an element via the Drag and <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="85ddba33-4573-4567-b9c6-6c2080aaf441">Drop</ac:inline-comment-marker> handler <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">in a view using </span></span></p><hr /><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Drag and drop a metadata element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's<span> </span></span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Drag and Drop handler" /></ac:link> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">on an <span>element symbol executing </span></span>the <strong>Apply Metadata</strong> command.</li></ul><h4>Applying annotation metadata to an element via the Textual Editor</h4><p>For other element annotation options, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Annotations" /></ac:link> page.</p><p>To apply annotation metadata to an element via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><p>Whether you want to apply annotation metadata with or without attributes, the overall procedure is generally the same as for:</p><ul><li data-uuid="b3929cec-c833-49a4-ae43-cb970c0ee418"><ac:link ac:anchor="Applying metadata with attributes to an element via the Textual Editor" /></li></ul><p>However, in this case:</p><ul><li data-uuid="36687bae-123b-4de6-a9ca-3d63f43dbfc6">The element to which the annotation metadata is applied must be declared separately.</li><li data-uuid="6a1e4c39-29b9-455a-a71b-d0a816a424a3">The applied metadata keyword is followed by the keyword <em>about </em>and the name of the element to which the annotation metadata is to be applied.  </li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="49a4e3ad-28ad-4d56-9f0f-3d2a7da484dd"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">system</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// element declaration</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">@</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Approval</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">about</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">system</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {  </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// annotation metadata applied to the element 'system' using the 'about' syntax</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approved</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">true</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approver</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">&quot;John Smith&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">/* or */</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">metadata</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Approval</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">about</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">system</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// annotation metadata applied to the element 'system' using the 'about' syntax</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approved</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">true</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">approver</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">&quot;John Smith&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577893 space=SYSML2P version=13 -->
## PAGE 00630: Model customization

- page_id: `249577893`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577893/Model+customization
- version_number: 13
- version_date: `2026-03-31T16:43:27.542+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Model customization allows you to customize certain predefined model components based on your needs and preferences. You can create model-based custom views with customized table columns or view palettes, customize symbolic view symbol styles, view creation dialogs, and validation suites with custom validation rules. Instead of, or in addition to, using the predefined components, you can model them yourself based on your project-specific needs.

The following model components are available for model-based customization.

###### Views customization

Model-based views customization is described under the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model composition' space=''] section's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Views' space=''] subsection:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom symbolic view definitions' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating custom symbolic view definitions' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom tabular view definitions' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating custom tabular view definitions' space='']

###### Symbol styles customization

Model-based symbol styles customization is described under the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model composition' space=''] section's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Views' space=''] subsection:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom style sheets' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extracting style sheets' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheets' space='']
    - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a style sheet condition' space='']
    - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating rules for a style sheet' space='']
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying a condition for a rule' space='']
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying styles for a rule' space='']
        - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating reusable styles' space='']
        - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Hiding style attributes from the Symbol Style dialog' space='']
      - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Style sheet styles tables' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assessing layout-sensitive changes' space='']

###### Validation suites and rules customization

Model-based validation suites and rules customization are described under the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Validation' space=''] section's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom validation suites and rules' space=''] subsection:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom validation suites and rules' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating validation suites and rules' space='']

###### Creation dialogs customization

Model-based creation dialogs customization is described under the section's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom creation dialogs' space=''] subsection:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom creation dialogs' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom View Creation dialog' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom Element Creation dialog' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom Relationship Creation dialog' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Model customization allows you to customize certain predefined model components based on your needs and preferences. You can create model-based custom views with c<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c023d75f-a74f-4269-b85c-62d2b8b74a81">ustomized table columns or view palettes,</ac:inline-comment-marker> customize symbolic view symbol styles, view creation dialogs, and validation suites with custom validation rules. Instead of, or in addition to, using the predefined components, you can model them yourself based on your project-specific needs.</p><p>The following model components are available for model-based customization. </p><h4>Views customization</h4><p>Model-based views customization is described under the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model composition" /></ac:link> section's <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Views" /></ac:link> subsection:</p><ul class="childpages-macro" style="text-align: left;"><li data-uuid="d95bf589-3172-46b6-8797-2211bf6aa33b"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Custom symbolic view definitions" /></ac:link><ul class="childpages-macro"><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating custom symbolic view definitions" /></ac:link></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="9b275611-bd8d-4cbe-b670-951febe1f0d2"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Custom tabular view definitions" /></ac:link><ul class="childpages-macro"><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating custom tabular view definitions" /></ac:link></li></ul></li></ul><h4>Symbol styles customization</h4><p>Model-based symbol <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576844 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
````
## PAGE 00631: Model management

- page_id: `249576844`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576844/Model+management
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2026-05-04T12:57:40.814+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Model management options

###### Containment tree

The Containment tree displays the hierarchy of your model elements, allowing you to review, organize, and modify the model.By default, the Containment tree has the Essential Elements Only mode enabled, displaying the model's essential elements most relevant to your modeling process. Disabling this option displays all model elements, including those less relevant to your modeling process. 
Learn more on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Containment tree' space=''] page.[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Textual Editor

Textual Editor is akin to a code editor allowing you to view and create your model using textual notation in a code-like format. The Textual Editor displays the model of a specific root namespace. 
Learn more on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Textual Editor' space=''] page. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Views

Views allow you to create a specific view of the model and create and modify model elements and their symbols. They do not display the model in its entirety with all of the elements the model contains. A view represents a particular set of model elements, encompassing a particular view of the model. 
Learn more on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with views' space=''] page. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3>Model management options</h3><h4>Containment tree</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The Containment tree displays <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the hierarchy of your model elements, allowing you to review, organize, and modify the model</span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">. </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">By default, the Containment tree has the Essential Elements Only mode enabled, displaying the model's essential elements most relevant to your modeling process. Disabling this option displays all model elements, including those less relevant to your modeling process. <br />Learn more on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Containment tree" /></ac:link> page.<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Containment_tree.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p><h4>Textual Editor</h4><p>Textual Editor is akin to a code editor allowing you to view and create your model using textual notation in a code-like format. The Textual Editor displays the model of a specific root namespace.<br />Learn more on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Textual Editor" /></ac:link> page.<br /><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-red-bolder,#c9372c);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="textual_notation_view.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p><h4>Views</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Views allow you to create a specific view of the model and create and modify model elements and their symbols. They do not display the model in its entirety with all of the elements the model contains. A view represents a particular set of model elements, encompassing a particular view of the model.<br />Learn more on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with views" /></ac:link> page.<br /><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-red-bolder,#c9372c);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="diagrams_view.png" /></ac:image></span></span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576816 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00632: Model Patch

- page_id: `249576816`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576816/Model+Patch
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-19T15:12:48.590+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Project management > Working with projects > Tools
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Model Patch technology allows you to take a part of a model (e.g., one package), export it to a patch file, and then bring that part of the model into another project or branch.

You can use the Model Patch mechanism:

- As an alternative for model merge when the source of truth is stored in the source version.
- For disconnected team collaboration when changes are made only in a certain scope, and it is not necessary to bring the whole project.
- To bring only necessary parts into the model rather than an entire used project.

To learn about the different types of model patches and how to use them, see the subpages in this section.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="309936d7-3604-489b-9b77-a054dd20a079">Model Patch</ac:inline-comment-marker> technology allows you to take a part of a model (e.g., one package), export it to a patch file, and then bring that part of the model into another project or branch.</p><p>You can use the Model Patch mechanism:</p><ul><li>As an alternative for model merge when the source of truth is stored in the source version.</li><li>For disconnected team collaboration when changes are made only in a certain scope, and it is not necessary to bring the whole project.</li><li>To bring only necessary parts into the model rather than an entire used project.</li></ul><p>To learn about the different types of model patches and how to use them, see the subpages in this section.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576817 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00633: Model Patch types

- page_id: `249576817`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576817/Model+Patch+types
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-17T14:41:11.011+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Project management > Working with projects > Tools > Model Patch
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When creating a model patch, you have two options to choose from: **All selected content**and **Changes from selected version to version #X**. This topic explains the difference between the two options and when to use each one.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Selecting a model patch type.

##### All selected content

Model patches of the *All selected content* type can be created from and applied to local, Teamwork Cloud, and **3D**EXPERIENCE platform projects.

Using this patch type, you can bring all the selected elements as they are. This patch type is always considered to be the source of truth, meaning that the patch content overrides your existing elements and/or adds elements that exist in the source version but not in the target.

Let's say that your source model contains the *Requirements* package with *maxFuelFlowSpeed* and *minFuelFlowSpeed* requirements, and your target model contains the *Requirements* package with *maxFuelCapacity* and *minFuelCapacity* requirements. If you decide to apply a model patch of the **All selected content** type containing the *Requirements* package to the target model, the *maxFuelFlowSpeed* and *minFuelFlowSpeed* requirements are added to the target model, while the *maxFuelCapacity* and *minFuelCapacity* requirements are deleted. In other words, the whole *Requirements* package in the target model is overwritten with data from the patch.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Applying a model patch of the *All selected content* type.

##### Changes from selected version to version #X

You can only create a model patch of the **Changes from selected version to version #X** type from a Teamwork Cloud project. However, you can apply this type of model patch to any project.

If you do not need to override existing elements in the target model but want to add them as concurrent changes, choose to bring changes from the selected version when creating a patch

Let's say that your source model contains the *Requirements* package with *maxFuelFlowSpeed* and *minFuelFlowSpeed* requirements, and your target model contains the *Requirements* package with *maxFuelCapacity* and *minFuelCapacity* requirements. If you apply a model patch of the **Changes from selected version to version #X** type containing the *Requirements* package, the *maxFuelFlowSpeed* and *minFuelFlowSpeed* requirements are added from the patch while the *maxFuelCapacity* and *minFuelCapacity* requirements are retained the target model. In other words the target model data is not overwritten but complemented with data from the patch.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Applying a model patch of the *Changes from selected version to version #X* type.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>When creating a model patch, you have two options to choose from: <strong>All selected content </strong>and <strong>Changes from selected version to version #X</strong>. This topic explains the difference between the two options and when to use each one.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="patch_types_sysml_v2.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Selecting a model patch type.</h6><h3>All selected content</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7c883924-9107-43da-af1e-554ccb6e0234"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Model patches of the <em>All selected content</em> type can be created from and applied to local, Teamwork Cloud, and <strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE platform projects.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Using this patch type, you can bring all the selected elements as they are. This patch type is always considered to be the source of truth, meaning that the patch content overrides your existing elements and/or adds elements that exist in the source version but not in the target.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Let's say that your source model contains the <em>Requirements</em> package with <em>maxFuelFlowSpeed</em> and <em>minFuelFlowSpeed</em> requirements, and your target model contains the <em>Requirements</em> package with <em>maxFuelCapacity</em> and <em>minFuelCapacity</em> requirements. If you decide to apply a model patch of the <strong>All selected content</strong> type containing the <em>Requirements</em> package to the target model, the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><em>maxFuelFlowSpeed</em> and <em>minFuelFlowSpeed</em> requirements</span> are added to the target model, while the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><em>maxFuelCapacity</em> and <em>minFuelCapacity</em> requirements</span> are deleted. In other words, the whole <em>Requirements</em> package in the target model is overwritten with data from the patch.</span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="model_patch_all_selected_content.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Applying a model patch of the <em>All selected content</em> type.</h6><h3>Changes from selected version to version #X</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="281f347a-3092-4dc4-ac62-ff660a118565"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can only create a model patch of the <strong>Changes from selected version to version #X</strong> type from a Teamwork Cloud project. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">However, you can apply this type of model patch to any project.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If you do not need to override existing elements in the target model but want to add them as concurrent changes, choose to bring changes from the selected version when creating a patch</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Let's say that your source model contains the <em>Requirements</em> package with <em>maxFuelFlowSpeed</em> and <em>minFuelFlowSpeed</em> requirements, and your target model contains the <em>Requirements</em> package with <em>maxFuelCapacity</em> and <em>minFuelCapacity</em> requirements. If you apply a model patch of the <strong>Changes from selected version to version #X</strong> type containing the <em>Requirements</em> package, the <em>maxFuelFlowSpeed</em> and <em>minFuelFlowSpeed</em> requirements are added from the patch while the <em>maxFuelCapacity</em> and <em>minFuelCapacity</em> requirements are retained the target model. In other words the target model data is not overwritten but complemented with data from the patch.</span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="model_patch_changes_form_specific_version.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Applying a model patch of the <em>Changes from selected version to version #X</em> type.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577570 space=SYSML2P version=7 -->
## PAGE 00634: Modeling actions with swimlanes

- page_id: `249577570`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577570/Modeling+actions+with+swimlanes
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-12-17T15:40:49.680+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions > Swimlane grid
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The swimlane grid symbol allows you to model and organize actions based on the elements that are responsible for performing those actions. For more information on swimlanes, see the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Swimlane grid' space='']page.

Within the context of a part that owns or inherits a perform action, the perform action contains actions that are performed by the subparts owned or inherited by the same owner as the perform action. Thus, the swimlane grid symbol is displayed for the perform action, where each individual swimlane in the swimlane pool represents each subpart. You can use swimlanes to model actions and their sequencing based on the performers, i.e., subparts that perform each action. The subparts names are indicated in the swimlane headers.

###### The part*drone in any mission*performs the action*run mission*. The subpartsof*drone in mission*then perform appropriate subactionsof*run mission*. This is illustrated showing subpartsasswimlaneperformers. The subactionsof*run mission*are connected using succession connection used to control the actionsequence. The output of subactionsare connected by a succession flow connection to the input of another subactionsmeaning that the inputs continue to be consumed and the outputs continue to be produced as the actionexecutes as opposed to streaming flow (flow connection). The sequencing of actionusages is controlled using control nodes such as join, fork, merge, and decide.

##### Managing swimlanes

Prerequisites:

- The perform action whose swimlanes represent the subparts is displayed in the view.

###### Displaying swimlane grid and creating additional swimlanes

To display swimlane grid or create additional swimlanes

- To display the swimlane grid:
  - Right-click the perform action symbol and in the shortcut menu, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] , then Display Swimlanes . The swimlane grid is displayed with swimlanes (along with any actions and connectors that may already be defined in the model), representing subparts owned or inherited by the part that owns or inherits the perform action.

50%

part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'mission control center'; 
performaction'run mission'; 
}

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- To create additional swimlanes:
  - Select the header of an existing swimlane and click the + button on the left or the right side of the swimlane, to create a swimlane to the left or the right of the existing one. Creating a new swimlane creates a subpart for the part that owns or inherits the perform action, represented by the new swimlane.

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'mission control center'; 
performaction'run mission'; 
}

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'mission control center';part part1;// new subpart represented by the newly created swimlaneperformaction'run mission';}

If the part that owns or inherits the perform action owns or inherits parts that are not yet displayed as swimlanes, clicking the + button displays a dropdown menu with the following categories:

- Display that has the following options:
  - part <the name of the part that does not yet have the corresponding swimlane displayed in the view> . Click to create the corresponding swimlane.
  - All . Click to create the corresponding swimlanes for all parts that are not represented in the swimlane symbol.
- Create that has the following option:
  - part . Click to create a part, represented by a new swimlane.

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

50%

part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'mission control center'; 
part'weather station'; // subpart whose representative swimlane is not displayed yet, thus it is available in the Display dropdown menuperformaction'run mission';}

###### Displaying actions in swimlanes

To display actions in swimlanes

- Select one swimlane by right-clicking its header or all swimlanes by right-clicking the swimlane pool. In the shortcut menu, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='']**Display** , then Display Actions in the dropdown menu . The specific swimlane's shortcut menu contains the same commands as the part element symbol it represents.

###### Displaying parameters, connectors, or other features for element symbols in swimlanes

To display parameters, connectors, or other features for element symbols in swimlanes

- Right-click element symbol(s) in the swimlane(s). In the shortcut menu, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='']**Display** , then click the appropriate command in its dropdown menu.

##### Modeling actions with swimlanes

###### Creating actions in swimlanes

You may create actions and the appropriate subparts' perform actions via the Textual Editor and then display the actions in swimlanes via the procedure. However, you can create actions in swimlanes using the view palette. This way, the appropriate perform actions are automatically created for the subparts in whose representative swimlanes the actions are created.

To create actions in swimlanes

1. .
2. In the view palette, under the Actions group, click the button for the needed action.
3. Click on the swimlane representing the part that you want to perform the action. Indicate the start and end of an action sequenceusing start and done actions. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Start and done actions' space=''] page.Connect and sequence actions using successions, flows, and succession flows. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action succession and flow' space=''] page.Control the sequencing of actions using control nodes. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Control nodes' space=''] page.Start and done actions, as well as control nodes, are not performed, thus parts in whose representative swimlanes they are displayed, do not have perform actions for performing them. These actions and nodes are owned by the perform action that owns the swimlane grid symbol, while their symbols are owned by the swimlane grid symbol.

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'mission control center'; 
performaction'run mission'; 
}

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone{ 
perform'run mission'.'check battery';// perform action with applicable feature chain automatically created for the part once theaction 'check battery'was created in the swimlane that represents the part 'drone' 
} 
part'mission control center'{ 
perform'run mission'.'start mission'; // perform action with applicable feature chain automatically created for the part once theaction 'start mission' was created in theswimlane that represents the part 'mission control center' 
} 
performaction'run mission'{ 
action'start mission'; // action created via view 
action'check battery'; // action created via view 
first'start mission'then'check battery'; // succession created via view 
} 
}

###### Deleting actions in swimlanes

To delete actions in swimlanes

- To delete an action element or symbol in swimlane, see the procedure [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space=''] page. Deleting an action element via swimlane deletes the appropriate perform action from the part responsible for performing the deleted action. Any associated connectors are deleted as well.

###### Changing which parts are responsible for performing actions via swimlanes

You can move actions between swimlanes to change which parts are responsible for performing these actions. The responsible parts are indicated in the header of the swimlane with the keyword «performer».

To change which parts are responsible for performing actions via swimlanes

- Click and drag the action symbol itself from its source swimlane to the target swimlane of the needed perform action.

**Details regarding the change:**

- If the source and target swimlanes are in the same swimlane grid symbol owned by the common perform action, m oving an action from one swimlane to another updates which part is the owner of the perform action responsible for performing the moved action.

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone{ 
perform'run mission'.'start mission'; // perform action is owned by the part 'drone' 
} 
part'mission control center'; 
performaction'run mission'{ 
action'start mission'; 
} 
}

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'mission control center'{ 
perform'run mission'.'start mission'; // perform action's owner is updated to the part 'mission control center' 
} 
performaction'run mission'{ 
action'start mission'; 
} 
}

- If the source and target swimlanes are in different swimlane grid symbols owned by different perform actions (see example below), m oving an action updates:
  - the moved action's owner to the new perform action;
  - which part is the owner of the perform action responsible for performing the moved action;
    - the feature chain for the referenced feature of the appropriate perform action .
- The same applies ifthe moved action is not inherited or owned by the perform action that owns the target swimlane.

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

part'drone mission'{ 
part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'mission control center'{ 
perform'run mission'.'start mission'; // the part 'mission control center' is the owner of the perform action responsible for performing the action 'start mission';the feature chain for the referenced action is 'run mission'.'start mission' 
} 
performaction'run mission'{ 
action'start mission'; // the owner of the action 'start mission' is the perform action 'run mission' 
} 
} 
part'drone in delivery mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'distribution center'; 
performaction'run mission'; 
} 
}

50%

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

part'drone mission'{ 
part'drone in any mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'mission control center'; // the previously owned perform action is moved out 
performaction'run mission'; // the previously owned action 'start mission' is moved out 
} 
part'drone in delivery mission'{ 
partdrone; 
part'distribution center'{ 
perform'run delivery mission'.'start mission'; // the part 'distribution center' is updated as the owner of the perform action responsible for performing the action 'start mission';the feature chain for the referenced action is updated to 'run delivery mission'.'start mission' 
} 
performaction'run delivery mission'{ 
action'start mission'; // the owner of the action 'start mission' is updated to the perform action 'run delivery mission' 
} 
} 
}

###### Deleting parts responsible for performing actions via swimlanes

To delete parts responsible for performing actions via swimlanes

1. Select the header of the swimlane representing the part you want to delete.
2. Follow the procedure [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space=''] page.

Deleting parts responsible for performing actions via swimlanes also deletes perform actions contained by the deleted part.

###### Adapting actions to specific context using swimlanes

You can adapt the existing actions to specific contexts without affecting the already modeled elements. You can do this by:

1. First, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Subsetting' space=''] the part that owns the perform action, which in turn owns the actions performed by the subparts. The subsetting part inherits the features of the subsetted part, including the subparts and the perform action.
2. You can then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Redefinition' space=''] the inherited perform action to adapt the subsetting part and its inherited features to the new context.

Then you can:

1. relevant to this context, which creates subparts for the subsetting part. You can then .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Redefinition' space=''] . You can then manage actions in these swimlanes: To easily redefine the inherited subparts via the swimlane headers, right-click the header of the swimlane that represents the subpart you want to redefine. In the shortcut menu, click**Refactor**, then**Redefine**. If an action is not owned but simply referenced by the perform action that is an inherited feature of the part represented by the swimlane, the action is displayed with the ^ prefix. See example below.
  1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Redefinition' space=''] the actions in these swimlanes via the Textual Editor.
  2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space=''] action symbols that are unnecessary in this context.
  3. that would then be owned by the redefining perform action of the subsetting part.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The part*drone in delivery mission*is created by subsetting the part *drone in any mission*(the example of which is displayed at the top of this page). It inherits the perform action*run mission*. However, since additional procedures should be involved, the inherited perform action*run mission*needs modifications within the context of the part*drone in delivery mission*. This is achieved by redefining the perform action*run mission*specifically for delivery missions. This allows introducing new actions relevant to delivery missions without impacting the *drone in mission*part.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The swimlane grid symbol allows you to model and organize actions based on the elements that are responsible for performing those actions. For more information on swimlanes, see the </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Swimlane grid" /></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> page.</span></p><p><span>Within the context of a part that owns or inherits a perform action, the perform action contains actions that are performed by the subparts owned or inherited by the same owner as the perform action. Thus, the swimlane grid symbol is displayed for the perform action, where each individual swimlane in the swimlane pool represents each subpart. You can use swimlanes to model actions and their sequencing based on the performers, i.e., subparts that perform each action. The subparts names are indicated in the swimlane headers.</span></p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="swimlanes_example.png" /></ac:image><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The part</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">drone in any mission</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> performs the action</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">run mission</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. The subparts</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> of </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">drone in mission</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> then perform appropriate subactions</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> of </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">run mission</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. This is illustrated showing subparts</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> as </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">swimlane </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">performers. The subactions</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> of </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">run mission</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> are connected using succession connection used to control the action</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> sequence. The output of subactions</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> are connected by a succession flow connection to the input of another subactions</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> meaning that the inputs continue to be consumed and the outputs continue to be produced as the action</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> executes as opposed to streaming flow (flow connection). The sequencing of action</span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> usages is controlled using control nodes such as join, fork, merge, and decide.</span></h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Managing swimlanes</h3><p>Prerequisites:</p><ul><li><span>The perform action whose swimlanes represent the subparts is </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSML2P/Displaying+elements+on+symbolic+views">displayed</a><span> in the view.</span></li></ul><h4>Displaying swimlane grid and creating additional swimlanes</h4><p>To display swimlane grid or create additional swimlanes</p><hr /><ul><li>To display the swimlane grid:<ul><li>Right-click the perform action symbol and in the shortcut menu, click <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Display]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong>, then<strong> Display Swimlanes</strong>. <br />The swimlane grid is displayed with swimlanes (along with any actions and connectors that may already be defined in the model), representing subparts owned or inherited by the part that owns or inherits the perform action.</li></ul></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="section" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e2f968a2-840f-4ba9-8467-005727ab4610"><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cf1e5dee-1704-4936-9bae-47360ce10218"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">50%</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f51f2efa-b47a-45d5-9252-1f165ba7c0d3"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'drone in any mission'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span>part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">drone</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'mission control center'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">perform</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">action</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'run mission'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="edac3faa-c46a-4f43-a6de-70f1859d22a4"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">50%</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="display_swimlanes.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ul><li>To create additional swimlanes:<ul><li>Select the header of an existing swimlane and click the <strong>+</strong> button on the left or the right side of the swimlane, to create a swimlane to the left or the right of the existing one.<br />Creating a new swimlane creates a subpart for the part that owns or inherits the perform action, represented by the new swimlane.</li></ul></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="section" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71571227-18f7-45f6-8da7-09a336695ef6"><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="381ca242-186c-4d52-934b-577e4461f01d"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">50%</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_swimlane.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9db1b273-2720-4239-907e-028ce2a437e7"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span
````
